title: The page starts listening
checkLabels.t8: Both of them have a name
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the fields too.
why.untouched: This is exactly the code I gave you. Nothing has been changed.
hint2.untouched: Both fields are there. What they lack is a wrapper, names and a button.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. <input> does not count — it has no closing tag.
hint2.not-closed: <form> wants a </form>. <button> wants a </button>. Check both.
why.lost: You deleted the heading. The task was about the fields.
why.lost-input: You lost a field. There are two — one for the name, one for the email. Both stay.
why.no-form: The fields are scattered across the page. They do not know they belong together — there is no way to send them at once. Wrap them in <form>.
hint2.no-form: A form is a wrapper, like a <ul> around items. It starts before the first field and ends after the button.
hint3.no-form: A similar case: <form action="/search" method="get"><input type="text" name="q"><button>Search</button></form>.
hint4.no-form: Open <form> above the first paragraph and close it below the button.
why.input-outside: A field has been left outside the form. Whatever is outside is not sent — as if it were not there.
hint2.input-outside: Check where you close the </form>. Close it after the first field and the second stays outside.
why.no-name: A field with no name, or an empty one. You are sending two texts, but nobody knows which is the name and which is the email. name="" is the same as no name at all — exactly the mistake that does not crash.
hint2.no-name: name is an attribute, inside the tag: <input type="text" name="...">.
hint3.no-name: A similar case: <input type="text" name="city">. The word in the quotes is the label on the parcel.
hint4.no-name: Name the fields after what they hold. And take care: the two names must be DIFFERENT. The same name on both fields means one eats the other.
why.one-name: Only one field has a name. Both need one — and they must differ.
hint2.one-name: The second field is for the email. It wants a name of its own.
why.no-action: The form does not know where the data goes. action is the address that will receive it.
hint2.no-action: The address does not exist yet and that is fine. Write /send.
why.no-method: The form does not know how to send the data. method says that: post or get.
hint2.no-method: A simple rule: search — get. Everything else — post. This is a message, not a search.
why.no-button: There is no submit button inside the form. The form collects data and has no way to hand it over.
hint2.no-button: <button type="submit">…</button>. Its place is inside the form, at the bottom.
why.empty-button: The button is empty. It should say what it does — Send, Send message.
hint2.empty-button: An empty button is a button nobody knows the purpose of. A blind visitor hears only button.
blocks.0.text: Until now your page only talked. It showed things to a person. Now it will start listening.
blocks.1.text: You added two fields to type in. Run it. They appear, you can type in them. And then? Nothing. Whatever you write stays there.
blocks.4.text: <form> wraps the fields and makes them one. Without it they are scattered boxes that know nothing about each other.
blocks.6.text: action says WHERE the data goes. The address of a server that will receive it.
blocks.7.text: method says HOW. post — the data travels hidden. get — it goes into the address, visible in the browser bar.
blocks.8.text: A simple rule: search — get. Everything else — post. A password does not belong in the address bar.
blocks.10.text: Every field must have a name. Without it, its data is not sent at all — as if the field were not there. And the two names must differ: the same name on two fields means one eats the other.
blocks.11.text: Think about what arrives on the other side: two texts. Which is the name, which is the email? name is the label on the parcel.
blocks.12.text: A forgotten name is a mistake that does not crash. The form submits, the person sees thank you, and the field is simply missing from your inbox. An empty name="" does exactly the same.
blocks.14.text: Build the form, press the button. Nothing will happen.
blocks.15.text: The address in action does not exist. And even if it did, someone must be waiting on the other side — a program on a server that takes the data, checks it, stores it and sends an email.
blocks.16.text: That is not HTML. That is another language, another course.
blocks.17.text: HTML builds the hand that offers. What happens to what is offered is not its business.
blocks.18.text: But the hand has to be built right. Otherwise nobody will take it.
blocks.19.text: Your turn. Make a form out of the two fields, give each one a name and add a button.
